'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of [[:en:Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|Tsukuriyama Kofun]] and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of [[:en:Sciadopitys verticillata|kōyamaki]]. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the [[:en:Yamato Kingship|Yamato court]].

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|en|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|WD=Q11639745|lt=Tsukuriyama Kofun}} and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=kōyamaki}}. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}.

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan}}} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|en|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|WD=Q11639745|lt=Tsukuriyama Kofun}} and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=kōyamaki}}. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court}}.

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}.

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|en|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|WD=Q11639745|lt=Tsukuriyama Kofun|1=Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)}} and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}.

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]
Sakakiyama Kofun was constructed in the southern part of Okayama Prefecture, on a hill west of the {{ill|Ashimorigawa River|ja|足守川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashimorigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 笹ヶ瀬川|1=Sasagase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11602473}}. It is located in front of the front projection of {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|en|Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)|WD=Q11639745|lt=Tsukuriyama Kofun|1=Tsukuriyama Kofun (Okayama)}} and, along with five other mounds, types a cluster of satellite tombs. Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}.

The tumulus was Historic site a National Historic site in 1921 (Taishō 10) as part of the "Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6" cluster.

== History ==
* 1912 (Meiji 45): Looting of Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) and Sakakiyama Kofun.
* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
* 1921 (Taishō 10): Designated a National Historic site.

== Related Facilities ==
* Okayama City Tsukuriyama Kofun Visitor Center (Okayama City, Kita Ward, Shinjo-shimo)

'''Sakakiyama Kofun''' (榊山古墳, Tsukuriyama No. 1 Kofun) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} located in Shinjo-shimo, Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. The tumulus is round in type and is part of the {{ill|Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|ja|造山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukuriyama Kofun Cluster|qq=}}. It is Historic site as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}} ("Tsukuriyama Kofun No. 1 to No. 6").

== Overview ==

[[File:出土地不明 馬形帯鉤.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Reference: Unprovenanced horse-shaped belt hook}} {{small|Similar to the horse-shaped belt hook attributed to Sakakiyama Kofun. Okayama Prefectural Museum Collection, photographed during an exhibition at Okayama City Museum.}}]]

Among these tombs, it is the closest to Tsukuriyama Kofun and situated at the highest elevation. Though the mound was looted in 1912 (Meiji 45), it has not been typeally excavated.

The mound appears circular, measuring approximately 35 meters in diameter. {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and earthenware fragments have been found on the surface. The burial facility features a {{ill|split-bamboo coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/split-bamboo coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} made of {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}. Due to looting, the mound’s central peak remains collapsed. burial goods attributed to the wooden coffin include six horse-shaped belt hooks, divine beast mirrors, dragon-pattern openwork fittings, perforated bells, and whetstones. Some burial goods, such as the belt hooks potentially crafted in the southern Korean Peninsula and other items originating from northern China, indicate an international connection.

The tomb dates to the early 5th century during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. It is believed to have been constructed after Tsukuriyama Kofun and along with the other satellite tombs. While Senzoku Kofun (No. 5) points to connections with Kyushu, Sakakiyama Kofun reflects ties to the Korean Peninsula, suggesting that the regional ruler buried in Tsukuriyama Kofun had an independent network beyond the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|WD=Q2658252|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato Kingship}}.

* 1913 (Taishō 2): Survey conducted by Chikichi Wada, reported in 1919.
